n: The discovery of penicillin
fa: Alexander Fleming noticed mould had killed bacteria in a petri dish he had accidentally left uncovered, leading to the first antibiotic

n: Edward Jenner and the first vaccine
fa: Jenner used cowpox material to protect a boy from smallpox, laying the foundation for modern vaccination

n: The discovery of X-rays
fa: Wilhelm Rontgen noticed a mysterious glow from a screen near his experimental tube and used the new rays to image his wife's hand

n: Ignaz Semmelweis and handwashing
fa: Semmelweis drastically cut childbed fever deaths by having doctors wash their hands, though his ideas were mocked by colleagues at the time

n: The first successful blood transfusion between humans
fa: James Blundell performed the first documented human-to-human transfusion to treat a woman after childbirth

n: The discovery of anesthesia
fa: William Morton demonstrated ether anesthesia during a public surgery in Boston, transforming surgical practice overnight

n: John Snow and the Broad Street pump
fa: Snow traced a London cholera outbreak to a single contaminated water pump, founding modern epidemiology

n: The discovery of insulin
fa: Frederick Banting and Charles Best isolated insulin, turning type 1 diabetes from a fatal diagnosis into a manageable condition

n: Marie Curie and mobile X-ray units
fa: Curie developed mobile X-ray vehicles nicknamed 'petites Curies' to treat wounded soldiers during World War I

n: The first successful organ transplant
fa: Joseph Murray performed the first successful kidney transplant between identical twins in Boston

n: The eradication of smallpox
fa: Smallpox became the first human disease ever declared eradicated, following a global vaccination campaign

n: The discovery of DNA's structure
fa: Watson and Crick's model relied heavily on X-ray images taken by Rosalind Franklin, whose contribution was underrecognized for decades

n: The first heart transplant
fa: Christiaan Barnard performed the first human heart transplant in South Africa; the patient survived 18 days

n: The invention of the stethoscope
fa: Rene Laennec rolled a sheet of paper into a tube to listen to a patient's heart without direct contact, embarrassed to place his ear on a woman's chest

n: The discovery of blood types
fa: Karl Landsteiner identified the ABO blood group system, explaining why some transfusions had been fatal

n: The first successful vaccine trial for polio
fa: Jonas Salk's polio vaccine trial involved over a million children known as the 'Polio Pioneers'

n: Florence Nightingale and modern nursing statistics
fa: Nightingale used pioneering data visualizations to show that poor sanitation killed more soldiers than battle wounds

n: The discovery of germ theory
fa: Louis Pasteur's experiments helped establish that microorganisms, not 'bad air', caused many diseases

n: The first successful cataract surgery
fa: Ancient physician Sushruta described early cataract couching surgery in Sanskrit medical texts

n: The discovery of the circulatory system
fa: William Harvey correctly described blood circulation, overturning centuries of accepted medical theory

n: The first successful appendectomy
fa: Claudius Amyand performed the first recorded successful appendix removal, operating on an 11-year-old boy

n: The discovery of nitrous oxide's pain-relieving effect
fa: Humphry Davy noted the effects of nitrous oxide, though it took decades before it was used in surgery

n: The first vaccine against rabies
fa: Louis Pasteur treated a boy bitten by a rabid dog with an experimental vaccine, saving his life

n: The discovery of vitamins
fa: Casimir Funk coined the term 'vitamine' after identifying substances that prevented diseases like beriberi

n: The Nobel-winning discovery of the cause of stomach ulcers
fa: Barry Marshall drank a solution containing bacteria to prove they, not stress, caused stomach ulcers

n: The first pacemaker implant
fa: The first implantable pacemaker was fitted in Sweden, though it only lasted a few hours before failing

n: The first recorded successful separation of conjoined twins
fa: One of the earliest recorded successful separations of conjoined twins was documented in Switzerland

n: The discovery of antiseptic surgery
fa: Joseph Lister introduced carbolic acid to sterilize surgical instruments, dramatically reducing post-surgical infections

n: The first permanent artificial heart
fa: Barney Clark became the first person to receive a permanent artificial heart, surviving 112 days

n: The discovery of the placebo effect's power
fa: Henry Beecher's influential paper highlighted how inactive treatments could still produce measurable improvements in patients

n: The first partial face transplant
fa: Isabelle Dinoire received the world's first partial face transplant in France after a severe injury

n: The discovery of the oral polio vaccine
fa: Albert Sabin developed an oral polio vaccine, making mass immunization campaigns far easier to administer

n: The identification of Alzheimer's disease
fa: Alois Alzheimer first described the brain changes in a patient with severe memory loss and confusion

n: Fleming's own warning about antibiotic resistance
fa: Alexander Fleming warned in his Nobel lecture that misuse of penicillin could lead to resistant bacteria

n: The first modern hand transplant
fa: The first successful modern hand transplant was performed in France, though the patient later had it removed

n: The discovery of hormones
fa: Bayliss and Starling identified secretin, coining the term 'hormone' for chemical messengers in the body

n: The first use of ultrasound in pregnancy
fa: Ian Donald adapted industrial sonar technology to safely image fetuses in the womb

n: The introduction of the measles vaccine
fa: The measles vaccine dramatically reduced a disease that once killed millions of children yearly

n: The discovery of the link between smoking and cancer
fa: A landmark study by Doll and Hill provided strong statistical evidence connecting smoking to lung cancer

n: The first laparoscopic surgery
fa: The first laparoscopic appendectomy was performed in Germany, pioneering minimally invasive surgery

n: The discovery of sickle cell disease's genetic basis
fa: Linus Pauling identified sickle cell disease as the first known 'molecular disease', tied to abnormal hemoglobin

n: The first baby born via in vitro fertilization
fa: Louise Brown became the first baby born via IVF, a medical breakthrough that once faced public controversy

n: The discovery of helicobacter pylori's role in ulcers
fa: The bacteria proven to cause ulcers later won its discoverers the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine

n: The development of Salvarsan, an early chemotherapy
fa: Paul Ehrlich developed Salvarsan, an early treatment for syphilis often called the first modern chemotherapy drug

n: The discovery of the cause of scurvy
fa: James Lind conducted one of the first controlled clinical trials, showing citrus fruits cured scurvy in sailors

n: The first human lung transplant
fa: James Hardy performed the first human lung transplant, though the patient survived only 18 days

n: The discovery of leptin and appetite regulation
fa: Researchers identified leptin, a hormone that helps regulate hunger and body weight

n: The historical use of maggot therapy
fa: Military surgeons observed that wounds infested with certain fly larvae healed cleaner, a technique still used today for some wounds

n: The discovery of the cause of pellagra
fa: Conrad Elvehjem identified niacin deficiency as the cause of pellagra, a once-widespread and often misunderstood disease

n: The first successful kidney dialysis
fa: Willem Kolff built the first working dialysis machine using sausage casings and a washing machine drum during wartime shortages

n: The discovery of the cause of beriberi
fa: Christiaan Eijkman found that unpolished rice prevented beriberi, an early clue leading to the discovery of vitamins

n: The first successful bone marrow transplant
fa: E. Donnall Thomas performed an early successful bone marrow transplant between identical twins

n: The identification of the cause of yellow fever
fa: Walter Reed's team confirmed that mosquitoes, not direct contact, spread yellow fever

n: The discovery of anesthesia's use in dentistry
fa: Horace Wells was among the first to use nitrous oxide during a dental extraction, though a public demonstration initially failed

n: The first successful cochlear implant
fa: An early cochlear implant restored partial hearing to a patient, paving the way for modern hearing implants

n: The discovery of the cause of Legionnaires' disease
fa: Investigators identified a new bacterium behind a mysterious outbreak at a Philadelphia convention, naming it Legionella

n: The first randomized controlled drug trial
fa: A British trial of streptomycin for tuberculosis is often cited as the first modern randomized controlled clinical trial

n: The discovery of the cause of puerperal fever's spread
fa: Alexander Gordon first suggested doctors themselves were spreading childbed fever between patients, decades before Semmelweis
